Commentary on the Prophet Jeremiah

Prologue of St. Thomas

2 Maccabees 15:14

This is the lover of his brethren, and of the people of Israel; this is he who prays much for the people and for the entire holy city, Jeremiah, the prophet of God.

These are the words of Onias the high priest to Judas Maccabees, commenting on the vision of Jeremiah. From this it is possible to derive four things regarding the present work, namely, the author, the subject matter, the method, and its usefulness.

Concerning the author, the present record delineates three things: his office, disposition, and activity.

With regard to his office, prophetic dignity is shown, as it says, Jeremiah, the prophet of the Lord.

With regard to internal disposition, his brotherly love, as it says, this is the lover of his brethren.

With regard to action, his dutiful compassion, as it says, this is he who prays much for the people and for the entire holy city.

Now, he is called the prophet of God to distinguish him from others, who are not prophets of God. For there are some who are prophets of the heavens, some who are prophets of the devil, and some who are prophets of God.

The prophets of the heavens are those who endeavor to tell the future from the figures of the heavens by the motions of the constellations; of these Isaiah speaks: let them stand and save you these augurs of the heavens, who contemplate the constellations and calculate the months, that from them they may announce to you the things which are to come (Isa 47:13). All divination belongs to this type of prophecy, which is accomplished through certain signs found in natural events.

Prophets of the devil are those who know certain future events from the revelation of demons, who, as Augustine says, gain knowledge by long experience, or perspicuity of nature, or by the revelation of supernal spirits. These are spoken of below: the prophets have prophesied by Baal and follow idols (Jer 2:8).

The prophets of God are those who, by the mediation of angels, as Dionysius says, receive an illumination from God concerning future events. For this reason, Cassiodorus says that prophecy is a divine inspiration announcing the outcome of events with immoveable truth. For this reason, it is said: you, child, shall be called the prophet of the Most High (Luke 1:76).

The first sort of prophecy is to be spurned, because it is false, and because it has no immoveable truth. For they receive the contingency of the effect from proximate causes, not necessity from first causes. And, therefore, it is said below: be not afraid of the signs of heaven (Jer 10:2).

The second type of prophecy is to be avoided entirely, because it is deceitful and pernicious. False prophets and unclean spirits I will drive from the land (Zech 13:2).

The third is to be followed and held as true, because it is illuminating. We have a more certain word of prophecy, to which you do well to attend as to a lamp shining in a dark place (2 Pet 1:19).

The holy prophets are called the prophets of God for three reasons.

First, because they are inspired by God. I will pour out my spirit upon all flesh, and your sons and your daughters will prophecy (Joel 2:28).

Concerning false prophets, on the contrary, who speak through their own spirit, it says: woe to foolish prophets who follow their own spirit, and see nothing (Ezek 13:3).

Second, because they are sent by God. Behold I send to you prophets and wise men and scribes (Matt 23:34). And now the Lord has sent me, and his spirit (Isa 48:16).

On the other hand, concerning false prophets it says below: I did not send prophets, yet they ran; I did not speak to them, yet they prophesied (Jer 23:21).

Third, because they bear witness to God. To this all the prophets bear witness (Acts 10:43). You are my witnesses (Isa 44:8).

His internal disposition is shown, burning with fraternal charity, as it says: this is the lover of his brethren (2 Macc 15:14).

Now the disposition of the prophets varies in three ways.

For some are lovers of profit. Sleeping and loving sleep; most unchaste dogs, they do not know abundance (Isa 56:10–11).

Some are lovers of human favor, of whom it says: they love the first seats in the feasts (Matt 23:6).

But the third are lovers of people, fulfilling what the Apostle says: may fraternal charity remain in you, and do not forget hospitality (Heb 13:1–2).

The first plunder riches. Thus says the Lord concerning the prophets who seduce my people, who bite with their teeth and preach peace (Micah 3:5). They dishonored me before my people for a handful of barley, and a crumb of bread, that they may kill souls which are not dying, and give life to souls which live not, lying to my people who believe their lies (Ezek 13:19).

The second deceive with flattering. His prophets have daubed them without tempering the mortar, seeing vain things and divining a lie unto them (Ezek 22:28).

But the third instruct with true doctrines. Below it says: I will give you shepherds after my own heart, and they will feed you with knowledge and understanding (Jer 3:15).

Moreover, in his action one sees his pious compassion for his people at: this is he who prays much (2 Macc 15:14).

His prayer is said to be much, or great, in three ways.

First, with regard to the depth of his contemplation. As it says below, remember that I stood in your sight, that I might speak good for them, and turn your indignation from them (Jer 18:20). To stand in his presence is to be present before him in contemplation.

Second, it was great because of the magnitude of his compassion. My bowels are disturbed, my liver is poured out on the earth (Lam 2:11).

Third, it was great with regard to duration of time, because it began before the captivity, and continued after. For this reason, James says: the constant prayer of the just man avails much (Jas 5:16).

And these three—namely, prophetic dignity, fraternal charity, and pious compassion—are necessary for a prophet.

Since, therefore, a prophet is established as a medium between God and the people—as it says, I was a mediator and stood in the midst between you and God (Deut 5:5)—it is fitting that through the gift of prophecy he should be joined to God—as it says, and through the nations she conveys herself to holy souls (Wis 7:27)—and by the bond of charity to be joined to the people—solicitously preserve the unity of spirit in the bond of peace (Eph 4:3). And so by praying he might bear the cause of the people to God, and by preaching bear the cause of God to the people.

Now from these three, there immediately appear another three.

For from the office of the man his method is clear, for he proceeds through similes and figures, which is the proper mode of the prophets. If any among you shall be a prophet of the Lord, I will appear to him in a vision, and I will speak to him through a dream (Num 12:6).

From his disposition, its usefulness is shown, as it is said: a faithful friend is a medicine of life and immortality (Sir 6:16). For it is our end to live well and to arrive at the glory of immortality.

The subject matter is clear from the action, for the subject according to the historical sense is the captivity of the people, which stirs up his prayer of compassion. For this reason, it is said: a soul in distress and an anxious spirit cry to you (Bar 3:1).

Preface of St. Jerome

Jeremiah the prophet, for whom this prologue is written, seems, in comparison with the Hebrews Isaiah, Hosea and certain others, to have been more rustic in his speech, though equal to them in sense, inasmuch as he prophesied by the same Spirit.

Further, the simplicity of his speech results from the place in which he was born, for he was from Anathoth, which to this day is a small village some three miles distant from Jerusalem. He was a priest, the son of priests, and sanctified in his mother’s womb, dedicating by his virginity the evangelical man to the Church of Christ.

He began to prophesy when still a boy, and he saw the captivity of Judah not only in the Spirit, but also with the eyes of the flesh. The Assyrians had already taken the ten tribes away to Media, and gentile colonists were now in possession of their land.

For this reason, he prophesied only against Judah and Benjamin, and composed an acrostic elegy consisting of four-verse strophes mourning the destruction of his city, which we have rendered with verses in a measured meter.

Moreover, we have corrected the order of the visions, which among the Greeks and the Latins has been entirely confused, back to their original exactness.

We have passed over the book of Baruch, which among the Hebrews is neither read, nor held to be canonical, standing ready because of these things for the curses of my adversaries, to whom it is necessary for me to respond for every little work I undertake. These things I suffer because you force me.

As for the rest, it was better that by my silence I should impose a limit on their fury, rather than provoke them to insane envy by writing some new thing every day.

St. Thomas’s Commentary on St. Jerome’s Preface

He who translated the book from Hebrew to Latin attached an introduction to the book of Jeremiah by which, in the oratorical manner, he accomplishes three things.

First, he renders his readers attentive;

second, he makes them docile to instruction, as it says, he began to prophesy when still a boy;

third, he makes them kindly disposed, as it says, which we have rendered with verses in a measured meter.

He renders them attentive in two ways:

first, from the profundity of the Scripture;

second, from the authority of the writer, where it says, a priest, the son of priests.

Concerning the first, he makes two observations.

First, he commends the profundity of the mystery, in which he equates himself to the other prophets, as he says, though equal to them in sense. And he shows the cause, namely the unity of the inspiring Spirit, as he says, inasmuch as he prophesied by the same Spirit. It is the spirit who speaks mysteries (1 Cor 14:2). The inspiration of the Almighty gives understanding (Job 32:8).

Second, he apologizes for his rustic speech when he says, further, the simplicity of his speech results from the place in which he was born. And therefore he himself confesses below: I do not know how to speak, for I am a boy (Jer 1:6).

The authority, however, of the writer is shown from three things.

First, from his ancestry and dignity: a priest, the son of priests: from which we learn that it was his duty to teach the law. The lips of the priest guard knowledge, and they seek the law from his mouth (Mal 2:7).

Second, from the privilege of sanctity: and sanctified in his mother’s womb. For this reason, it is said, before you came forth from the womb I sanctified you (Jer 1:5).

Third, from the integrity of the flesh, virginity, which is proven from what is said below: you shall not take a wife in this place, and you shall have no sons (Jer 16:2).

He began to prophesy when still a boy. Here he renders them docile to instruction both by marking out the time—he saw the captivity of the city not only in the Spirit, referring to the future, but also with the eyes of the flesh, which speaks of the present—and by determining the subject, namely, because he did not prophecy concerning the ten tribes, but only the two.

The Assyrians had already taken the ten tribes away to Media. For 2 Kings 17 makes clear that the ten tribes were taken captive by the king of the Assyrians in the time of Ahaz, king of Judah.

Colonists, that is, foreign-born settlers, were brought to cultivate the land.

His elegy, in Lamentations.

We have rendered with verses in a measured meter. Here, he makes them well-disposed to his person by three devices.

First, from the good things he has done without arrogance, saying, which we have rendered with verses in measured meter, that is, writing letters in order before each series of verses for Lamentations, and for the book of Jeremiah.

Second, he softens the reproach he has given, by excusing himself for his treatment of Baruch, whom he has omitted. We have passed over the book of Baruch, which among the Hebrews is neither read nor held to be canonical.

Third, he shows what difficulties press upon him from the objections of his rivals.

For this reason, he first shows their envy at this entire undertaking;

and second, he shows the cause of his having taken up this work, these things I suffer because you force me.

As for the rest—it is as though he were saying, “If it were not for your entreaties I would have been able to slip away bearing none of their fury.”

Chapter 1

Jeremiah becomes a prophet

Lecture 1

Title of the work

1:1 The words of Jeremiah, the son of Hilkiah, from the priests that were in Anathoth, in the land of Benjamin.

1. This book, after the manner of the other prophets, is divided into two parts:

namely, the title;

and the treatise which begins with, and the word of the Lord came to me, saying (Jer 1:4).

In the title, it does two things.

First, it describes the author;

and second, the time of the prophecy, at the word of the Lord which came to him (Jer 1:2).

The author is described in three ways.

First, by name, at the words of Jeremiah, which is translated as “highness to the Lord,” which is fitting for the eminence of his contemplation.

Second, from the prophetic line, the son of Hilkiah, which, according to the rule of Jerome, we are to understand as meaning that wherever the name of a prophet’s father is put in the title, he also was a prophet. And from the priestly line, from the priests, whose responsibility it was to know the Law: ask the law of the priest (Hag 2:12).

Third, he is described by his dwelling place: that were in Anathoth. For the Levites received in each tribe certain estates in which they were to dwell, as it says in Numbers 35. Concerning these words it is said, my words shall not fail (Isa 59:24).

Now, it is to be understood that some of the prophetic books are named from an interior action: whence it says, the vision of Isaiah or the vision of Obadiah; others from an exterior action, and these are called in the title a word, as is the case with this book; still others from the subject matter of a threat, and so are called in the title a burden. Habakkuk and Nahum are of this sort.

Lecture 2

Date of the work

1:2 The word of the Lord which came to him in the days of Josiah, the son of Amon, king of Judah, in the thirteenth year of his reign.

1:3 And which came in the days of Jehoiakim, the son of Josiah, king of Judah, until the completion of the eleventh year of Zedekiah, the son of Josiah, king of Judah, even until the deportation of Jerusalem in the fifth month.

2. Here, he describes the time of the prophecy, because he prophesied under the three kings spoken of in 2 Kings 20 in succession.

Above he said, words. Why does he now use the singular word?

In response, it must be said that while prophetic words are in themselves many, they are nonetheless one in their origin, because they come from the uncreated Word.

Yet, since that Word is eternal, as it says in John 1, how is it spoken of as made?

It is to be said that it is not said to be simply made, but made to someone; there was indeed no change in the Word, but in him to whom revelation was made by the Word, according to which sense God is said to have been made our refuge.

Also, one asks: why is there no mention of the other two kings, that is, Joathan, the son of Josiah, and Joachin, the son of Jehoiakim, who is also called by the other name Jekoniah.

It is to be said that both of these only reigned three months, as it is said in 4 Kings 24, and because Sacred Scripture does not care for minute details, they are reckoned with the times of the other kings.

In the fifth month, in which the city was destroyed, although it was captured in the fourth month, as it says below (Jer 39:2).

Lecture 3

Jeremiah’s office of prophet

1:4 And the word of the Lord came to me, saying:

1:5 before I formed you in the womb, I knew you; and before you came forth from the womb, I sanctified you and gave you as a prophet to the nations.

1:6 And I said: ah, ah, ah, Lord God, behold I do not know how to speak, for I am a boy.

1:7 And the Lord said to me: do not say, I am a boy, for to all that I shall send you, you shall go, and all whatsoever I shall command you, you shall say.

1:8 Do not be afraid at their presence, because I am with you to rescue you, says the Lord.

1:9 And the Lord sent forth his hand and touched my mouth, and said to me: behold I have given my words in your mouth.

1:10 See, I have established you this day over nations and over kingdoms, so that you may root up and pull down, lay waste and scatter, build and plant.

3. Here begins the discourse of the book itself.

First, the office of prophet is enjoined upon Jeremiah.

Second, a prophecy is given to the now instituted prophet, at and the word of the Lord came to me (Jer 2:1).

The former is divided into two.

In the first, the office is enjoined;

in the second, the manner of its execution is shown—as it says, you, therefore, gird up (Jer 1:17).

The former is divided into two.

In the first, he is established in the prophetic office;

in the second, the subject matter of the prophecy is delineated to him—as it says, and the word of the Lord came to me (Jer 1:11).

The former is divided into two.

In the first, the preparation of the prophet is related;

in the second, the conferral of the prophetic gift—as it says, and the Lord sent forth his hand (Jer 1:9).

The former is divided into three.

In the first is noted the preparation on the part of the Lord.

In the second, the objection of the prophet is mentioned at, and I said: ah, ah, ah (Jer 1:6).

In the third, the refutation of the objection at, and the Lord said (Jer 1:7).

Concerning the first, three things are to be said.

First, the preparation of the prophet is spoken of: the word of the Lord came to me, either externally through a subordinate creature, or internally through inspiration. I will hear what the Lord God says in me (Ps 84:8).

I formed you: within formative nature itself God makes a form, as the first cause within a second: with skin and flesh you have clothed me (Job 10:11). I knew you, with a knowledge of approval. The Lord called me from the womb, from the belly of my mother he has remembered my name (Isa 49:1).

Second, his privileged sanctification is mentioned: and before. They have mistreated him, who from his mother’s womb was consecrated a prophet (Sir 49:7).

Third, the predetermination of his office is noted. And gave you as a prophet, that is, in my disposition. I have given you as a light to the nations, that you should be my salvation to the ends of the earth (Isa 49:6).

Concerning his sanctification in the womb, let us see four things.

First, the time. And it seems that before the infusion of the soul, sanctification of this type is possible, as it says in the Gloss of Ambrose on Luke 1: there was not yet within him the spirit of life, and there was already present in him the spirit of grace.

Further, it says here: before I formed you in the womb, I knew you. Here, it is understood that he is speaking of spiritual knowledge, which is approbation. Therefore, before his formation he had grace.

On the other hand, it seems he did not before he was born from the womb, because Augustine says, no one can be reborn unless he has already been born. No one has grace unless he has been reborn, because he becomes the son of God through grace; therefore, he does not even have the grace of sanctification.

To this it is to be said that the sanctification about which we are speaking occurs through grace whose subject is the soul, and that, therefore, it cannot exist before the infusion of the soul. But here it happens by a special privilege after the infusion of the soul, but before he is born from the womb.

To the first point, one must say that here the spirit of life is externally called air, freely breathed. Alternatively, the spirit of life is said to be within when one notes if breath is drawn.

To the other point, it is to be said that what is understood by a notion of approbation is said in reference to predestination to grace, and not present justice.

As to the other point, one must say that no one is able to be reborn by general law, by that regeneration which comes through the sacraments before he is born, because he is not yet able to be subject to the operations of the ministers of the Church, through whom grace of this sort is dispensed. But God does not bind his power with the sacraments, and therefore he is able to sanctify someone in the womb by a special privilege.

Second, one asks, to whom is this sanctification fitting? It seems that it is fitting to Christ, of whom it is said in the gloss, that before he came forth from the womb he was sanctified in the belly.

On the contrary, however, to sanctify is to make holy what was unholy. But he was never unholy, therefore, etc.

Also, it seems that it is appropriate for inanimate objects, according to what is said in Psalm 45:5, the Most High has sanctified his tabernacle.

Yet on the contrary, things of this sort are not susceptible of grace.

It is to be said in response to the first point that something is said to be unholy in two ways: either privatively, and then the existence of the subject is needed, and such would not be fitting for Christ; or negatively, and in this sense, it is fitting for Christ to pass from unholy to holy, because, before he was man, he was not holy with regard to created holiness.

To the other point, one must say that in one way something is called holy which is dedicated to divine worship, and in this way animate and inanimate things can be sanctified. Sometimes, however, “holy” is used to mean faithful, or free from sin, or from the carnal act. Such sanctification comes through grace, and is only possible in men. But since all sanctification should be ordered to that most holy conception of the Son of God, it was fitting that the mother in whose womb the celebrated conception took place had been sanctified. John the Baptist, also, while yet in the womb pointed to another who was still in the womb. Jeremiah, too, who among others expressly treated of the manner of that most holy conception, below: the Lord has created a new thing upon the earth: a woman shall encompass a man (Jer 31:22).

Third, one asks about the effect of sanctification. It is to be understood that sanctification is ordered against the uncleanness of original sin, in which one has to consider the guilty state of lacking the vision of God, which arises from the lack of due justice. This he totally removes insofar as it pertains to the impediment of person, even though the price has not yet been paid by which the impediment of nature would be removed. One must also consider the tinder, so to speak, which is the inclination to every evil, which indeed remains as an obstructed condition not inclining to that towards which it is ordered. While in some it would incline to the venial, albeit not to the mortal, yet in the blessed Virgin, her inclination was towards neither of these.

Fourth, one must ask about the comparison of sanctifying grace and sacramental grace.

One must say that in a general manner sanctifying grace prevails, because the inclination to mortal sin remains after one receives sacramental grace. But in a certain respect, sacramental grace prevails with regard to the indelible mark it makes on the soul and other such things.

And I said. Here, the prophet raises an objection.

First, he expresses his astonishment: ah, because of the defects of his life; ah, because of his lack of knowledge; ah, because of his lack of eloquence. And I said, ah, ah, ah (Ezek 4:14).

Second, he gives the reason for his astonishment, and mentions his lack of eloquence: behold I do not know how to speak—I am not eloquent from yesterday or the day before (Exod 4:10)—and the fact of his youth, for I am a boy; therefore, my age is not appropriate to the office of preaching. I once hoped that a longer life would speak, and that a multitude of years would teach wisdom (Job 32:7). You who are older speak, for it is fitting for you (Sir 32:3).

For this reason, he refuses the office of preaching, to which Isaiah offered himself (Isa 6:8). Gregory shows this in the Gloss: some laudably seek the office of preaching, others are laudably forced into it. Because out of that love by which he was borne to God, he feared the loss of contemplation, and he, through love of his neighbor, desired the salvation of his neighbor. However, he did not offer himself before he was cleansed, neither did he stubbornly refuse.

And the Lord said. Here, he rejects this objection. And because he mentioned his youthfulness, he promises three things against the defects of young men.

For children are inconstant, and against this he promises constancy: do not say, excusing yourself, for to all, that is to be done. A man’s way is not his own, nor does it pertain to a man to walk and direct his steps (Jer 10:23). Think on him in all your ways, and he will direct your steps (Ps 3:6).

He also promises eloquence against the objection that children are without eloquence, and all whatsoever. Wisdom opens the mouth of the mute, and makes the tongues of infants articulate (Wis 10:21).

He promises courage against the timidity of children: do not be afraid at their presence, because I am with you to rescue you, this is not because you will be without difficulties, but because you will bear them all with patience. I will not fear evil, for you are with me (Ps 12:4).

And the Lord sent forth his hand. Here, he offers the gift of prophecy in the manner of a sacrament.

First, he places an action—the Lord sent forth his hand—in a vision of images, because a boy seeing human limbs would not have been terrified, by which is signified the liberality of God’s goodness. When you open your hand, all things will be filled with goodness (Ps 103:28). He is not, however, cleansed beforehand with a burning coal as was Isaiah, because he lacked sin on account of his youth and sanctification.

Second are placed the words: and said to me. These contain three things.

First, it expresses the gift: behold I have given. The Lord will give his word with much power to those announcing the good news (Ps 67:12).

Second, it shows his station: see, I have established you this day over nations, different ones, to which he preached, as a mediator between them and God. Behold, I have established you as a God to Pharaoh (Exod 7:1). You have established me as the head of nations (Ps 17:44).

Third, it explains his duty: so that you may root up, evil things with regard to the root; pull down, with regard to the contrivances of a wicked government; lay waste, the union of evil men; scatter, the defense of evil; build, good things with regard to right order; plant, with regard to the root. For they do not lack external fortifications such as a fence. He was consecrated a prophet, to overturn, to pull up, to destroy and to build again and to renew (Sir 49:9).

Lecture 4

Vision of the rod

1:11 And the word of the Lord came to me, saying: what do you see, Jeremiah? And I said: I see a rod watching.

1:12 And the Lord said to me: you have seen well, because I will keep watch over my word to do it.

4. Here, he determines the subject matter for which he is particularly established to prophesy, namely, the destruction of Jerusalem, and the captivity of the people.

First, he shows the certainty of the prophecy;

second, he shows the captivity itself, at and the word of the Lord came (Jer 32:26).

Concerning the first, there are three points.

First is the incitement of the prophet to see. What do you see? It is as though he were saying, “By this you are a prophet, it is your duty to see.” He who is now called a prophet was once called a seer (1 Sam 9:9).

Second is the vision: and I said: I see images in a vision: a rod, as it were, in the hand of the Lord ready to strike. The yoke of my iniquities has kept watch, they are wrapped up in his hand and put upon my neck (Lam 1:14). Some understand the rod watching as the rod of thieves inserted through windows to take plunder from houses, while they keep watch and others sleep. But the first interpretation is better.

Third is the explanation: and the Lord said: you have seen well. It is as though he were saying, “These things are in accord with the truth.” I will keep watch: I will be solicitous. The word which will come forth from my mouth (Isa 55:11).

Lecture 5

Evil from the north

1:13 And the word of the Lord came to me a second time saying: what do you see? And I said: I see a boiling cauldron, and its face is from the face of the north.

1:14 And the Lord said to me: from the north shall spread every evil upon the inhabitants of the land.

1:15 For, behold, I will call together all the families of the kingdoms of the north, says the Lord, and they shall come, and each one shall place his throne in the entry of the gates of Jerusalem, and upon all her walls round about, and upon all the cities of Judah.

1:16 And I will speak my judgments with them upon all the evil of those who have abandoned me, and poured out drink offerings to foreign gods, and worshiped the work of their own hands.

1:17 You, therefore, gird up your loins, arise and speak to them all which I command you. Do not be afraid in their presence, neither fear the countenance of their face.

1:18 I have surely set you today as a fortified city, and as an iron pillar, and as a brazen wall over all the land, for the kings of Judah and her princes, for her priests and for the people of the land.

1:19 And they shall wage war with you but will not prevail, for I am with you says the Lord, to deliver you.

5. Here, he describes the destruction of Jerusalem itself.

First, he speaks of the incitement to see, what do you see?, as above.

Second, he speaks of the vision: and I said: I see a boiling cauldron, which signifies the burning of the city. And its face—its bottom—is from the north, from which direction the fire was lit. Place an empty pot upon the coals, that it may grow hot, and melt its bronze, that its filthiness may flow into the midst thereof and its rust be consumed (Ezek 24:11).

Third is set down the exposition: and the Lord said.

And first, it is explained generally: from the north, that is, from the king of Babylon, who was towards the north with regard to the Holy Land. Inhabitants of the land, of Jerusalem, or of other adjacent cities, which will be destroyed by him. As it says below, behold, the sound of a noise comes, and a great shaking from the land of the north, that he may turn the cities of Judah into wilderness and the habitation of dragons (Jer 10:22).

Second, there follows the order of the whole matter.

First is given the assembling together of the army: for, behold, I will call together, by inspiration, the kingdoms subject to Nebuchadnezzar. He shall raise a sign to the nations that are afar off (Isa 5:26).

Second, he speaks of the siege of the city: and they shall come, and each one shall place his throne, that is, the tent, in the entry of the gates, it is as though they will bring the siege to the very walls. The horsemen shall put their seats in the gate, and the covering of Judah shall be revealed (Isa 22:7). Alternatively, he is thinking of the captivity of the city itself, and touches upon what happens below: and all the princes of the king of Babylon entered and sat in the middle gate (Jer 39:3).

Third, he discusses the punishment of the inhabitants: and I will speak my judgments, not with words, but with punishments. They shall judge you with the judgments of adulteresses, and of those who shed blood (Ezek 16:45).

You, therefore, gird up. Here, he teaches the manner in which this will be carried out:

and first, that it should follow immediately: gird up your loins, as one who prepares himself to go quickly. Gird up your loins as a man (Job 38:3).

Second, that he should proceed fearlessly.

Concerning this, he first excludes fear: do not be afraid. Do not fear them, neither be dismayed at their face, for it is a rebellious house (Ezek 3:9). Who are you that you fear mortal man, and the son of man, who is like straw that dries up and is forgotten by the Lord your maker, who stretches forth the heavens, and establishes the earth; and you have shrunk back in fear the whole day from the presence of him who troubles you, and had readied you for destruction (Isa 51:12).

Second, he promises the fortitude to resist: I have surely set you today as a fortified city, to uphold others, as an iron pillar. The Lord has given me a learned tongue, that I might know how to uphold him who is fallen with a word, that he may not flag within himself (Isa 50:4). And as a brazen wall. I have set your face as steel and as flint (Ezek 3:9).

Third, he promises the disappointment of the enemy: and they shall wage war with you, but will not prevail—they shall not be able to turn you from the truth. As it says below, I shall make you as a strong brazen wall before this people; and they shall wage war against you, and they shall not prevail, because I am with you to save you, and I will rescue you, says the Lord (Jer 15:20).

Collations

Concerning the things mentioned above, one must know first that God is able to have a knowledge (Jer 1:5) of approbation of something, as has been said. Of foresight: all things are known to the Lord God before they are created; thus also after their completion he sees all things (Sir 23:29). Of predestination: the Lord knows who are his (2 Tim 2:9). Of the infusion of grace: I know my sheep and they know me (John 10:27). Of special familiarity: and I have known you by name (Exod 33:12). Of glorification: I have known him in his blessings, and have given him an inheritance (Sir 44:25).

It is also to be noted that God touches (Jer 1:9) some by reproving them: have mercy on me, have mercy on me, at least you, O my friends, for the hand of the Lord has touched me (Job 19:21); by cleansing them from sin: Jesus touched him, saying, ‘I will it, be clean’ (Matt 8:3); by infusing grace: my beloved put forth his hand through the hole, and my belly trembled at his touch (Song 5:4); by strengthening with grace: and behold, a hand touched me, and raised me to my knees, and the knuckles of my hands (Dan 10:10); by stirring up fervor: touch the mountains and they shall smoke (Ps 143:5).

It is also to be noted that the sinful soul is called a cauldron (Jer 1:13) because of the fervor of its concupiscence. He makes the deeps of the sea to boil like a pot, and he reckons it as when they boil unguents (Job 41:22). Because of the carnality of its work: they break their bones, and cut them up finely as in a kettle, as meat in the midst of a cooking vessel (Mic 3:3). Because of the blackness of its stain: the face of all is as the blackness of a pot (Nah 2:10).

It is also to be noted that the aforementioned cauldron is heated by the fire of inordinate love. When the fire has been kindled and spread abroad, they will perish at the rebuke of your countenance (Ps 79:17). Of wrath and contention: behold you are all kindling the fire and girded about with flames (Isa 50:11). Of eternal damnation: a fire has been kindled in my wrath, and it shall burn to the lowest depths of hell (Deut 32:22).

It is also to be noted that the devil is called the north wind (Jer 1:14), because of the violence of his temptation. The voice of his thunder has struck the earth, a storm of the north wind and a gathering of air (Sir 43:18). Because he hinders the fruit of good work: arise, O north wind, and come, you south wind; blow through my garden, and its spices shall flow forth (Song 4:16). Because he dries the river of tears: the north wind scatters the rains, and a sad face a backbiting tongue (Prov 25:23).

It is also to be noted that the Church, and even the soul, is fortified (Jer 1:18) by divine assistance: I shall be a fire for them round about, and I shall be in the midst of him in glory (Zech 2:5); by the protection of the angels: his height is a rampart of stones (Isa 33:16); by the example of the holy fathers: the tower of David is your neck, which is built with bulwarks (Song 4:4); by the help of one’s brothers: a brother who is helped by his brother is like a strong city (Prov 18:19); by the remedy of the sacraments: I am a wall, my breasts are like a tower, from which I am made as one finding peace (Song 8:10).

Chapter 2

Israel’s idolatry condemned

Lecture 1

God’s love for Israel

2:1 And the word of the Lord came to me, saying:

2:2 Go, and cry in the ears of Jerusalem, saying: thus says the Lord: I have remembered you, and taken mercy on your youth, and the love of your betrothal, when you followed me in the desert, in a land which was not sown.

2:3 Israel was holy to the Lord, the first of his fruits; all who swallow him up transgress. Evil shall come upon them, says the Lord.

6. Here begins the prophecy of Jeremiah himself now established in the prophetic office; and it is divided into three parts.

In the first, he predicts the coming captivity;

in the second, in the book of Lamentations, he mourns the captivity that has already occurred;

in the third, in the book of Baruch, he leads those who have now been taken captive to punishment.

The first is divided into two parts.

In the first, he predicts the captivity, in a prophetic manner;

in the second, he relates it in a historical mode: Zedekiah was one and twenty years old when he began to reign (Jer 52:1).

The first is divided into two parts.

In the first, he prophesies to the people of the Jews;

in the second, to the various peoples of the nations, because he was also established as a prophet for them, as is shown from the preceding section and from what is said in chapter 46: the word of the Lord that came to Jeremiah the prophet against the Gentiles (Jer 46:1).

The first is divided into two parts.

In the first, a prophecy is given to the people who are to be taken captive before the captivity;

in the second, to the rest of the exiles after the captivity: in the ninth year of Zedekiah king of Judah (Jer 39:1).

The first is divided into two parts.

In the first, the preaching of Jeremiah is given;

in the second, the objection of his listeners: the word that came to Jeremiah from the Lord (Jer 34:1).

The first is divided into two parts.

In the first, he speaks of a threat;

in the second, of a consolation: this is the word which came to Jeremiah from the Lord, saying (Jer 30:1).

The first is divided into two.

In the first, he speaks of a true threat;

in the second, he excludes any false consolation: in the beginning of the reign of Jehoiakim, the son of Josiah, king of Judah (Jer 27:1).

The first is divided into two.

In the first, he mentions the threat against the people as a whole;

in the second, against the elders specifically: and Pashhur, the son of Emmer, the priest, who was appointed chief in the house of the Lord, heard Jeremiah prophesying these words (Jer 20:1).

The first is divided into two.

In the first, he makes a threat;

in the second, the prophet, as though feeling compassion, inserts his prayer: the word of the Lord which came to Jeremiah, concerning the words of the drought (Jer 14:1).

The first is divided into two parts.

In the first, he convicts them of their guilt through judgment;

in the second, he calls them back to repentance: it is said commonly (Jer 3:1).

The first is divided into two parts.

In the first, he gives the reason for such great respect, in that he does not immediately bring vengeance upon their guilt, but contends with them in judgment, that he may call them back;

in the second, he follows the order of judgment when he says, hear the word of the Lord, O house of Jacob (Jer 2:4).

Regarding the first, there are two points.

First, he deduces the reason from God’s compassion: cry, that is, openly proclaim, in the ears of Jerusalem, that is, to her inhabitants.

He also mentions the three things which move God to compassion.

First is the purity of their age: on your youth, in which spouses love each other more vehemently, when you followed, as they were coming out of Egypt. And he will sing there as in the days of your youth, and as in the days when you came up out of the land of Egypt (Hos 2:15).

Second, he recalls the memory of his original love: and the love, namely, that by which I have betrothed you to myself in giving the Law. As it says below: with an everlasting love have I loved you; therefore have I drawn you with mercy (Jer 31:3).

Third, the obedience of their work: when you followed me in the desert, in a land which was not sown, which cannot be sown. For this reason, you did not draw back even though the way was difficult, although some grumblers did say: you have led us into this terrible place, which cannot be sown (Num 20:5).

Second, he cites the reason as coming from the dignity of the people.

First, he places the dignity of the people: holy, as though it was dedicated to the worship of the Lord, and consecrated to the Lord. The first of his fruits, because the Lord has chosen you from all peoples, as the first fruits. The Lord’s portion is his people; Jacob is the rope of his inheritance (Deut 32:9). As the first fruits of a fig tree in its upper branches, I saw their fathers (Hos 9:10).

Second, he excludes the threat of the enemy: all who swallow him up, in accordance with the law found in Leviticus 7, that it was only permitted for the priests to eat the first fruits. Who devour my people as a morsel of bread (Ps 13:4).

Lecture 2

The sins of the fathers

2:4 Hear the word of the Lord, O house of Jacob, and all the families of the house of Israel.

2:5 Thus says the Lord: what iniquity did your fathers find in me? For they have departed from me, and walked after vanity, and have become empty.

2:6 And they did not say: where is the Lord, who made us to go up from the land of Egypt, who led us through the desert, and through an uninhabitable and trackless land, through a land of thirst, and the image of death, through a land in which no man has walked, and no man dwelt?

2:7 And I brought you into the land of Carmel, that you should eat its fruit and the good things thereof; and when you entered you polluted my land, and turned my inheritance into an abomination.

2:8 My priests have not said: where is the Lord? And those who hold the law do not know me, and the shepherds have rebelled against me, and the prophets have prophesied by Baal, and followed idols.

7. Here he follows the order of judgment:

and he speaks first of the accusation;

second, he refutes their excuse, at but I have planted you as my chosen vine, every seed true (Jer 2:21).

The first point is divided into three.

First, he heaps up their offences as stemming from ingratitude for all the good they have received;

second, from the enormity of their misdeed, where he says, therefore, I shall still contend with you in judgment (Jer 2:9);

third, from the stubbornness of their minds, when he says, is Israel not my servant, or my home-born slave? (Jer 2:14).

Concerning the first, there are three points.

First, God absolves himself of guilt, lest the cause of their ingratitude should seem to lie with him: what iniquity did your fathers find in me? Certainly nothing bad. They have departed—they have abandoned him for a long time; vanity—idols, which are of no benefit to those who worship them. O my people, what have I done to you, how have I been troublesome to you, answer me (Mic 6:3).

Second, he mentions the abundance of his kind deeds, touching especially on the exodus from Egypt. He treats of this kindness in three ways.

With regard to the place from which he led them: and they did not say: where is the Lord? They sought me with their whole heart. To go up, because of the site of the land. As it says below, I led them from the land of Egypt (Jer 11:4).

With regard to the way by which he led them: who led us through the desert . . . the image of death, because death was threatened by the very sight of it: and he was your leader in the great and terrible wilderness (Deut 8:15).

With regard to the land into which he led them: and I brought you into the land of Carmel, because it symbolizes fertility, not because that mountain is in the land of promise. The land around which we have gone is very good (Num 14:7).

Third, he mentions the fault of ingratitude.

First, how it extends to everyone commonly: and when you entered you polluted with idols. The land is stained with blood, and polluted by their deeds (Ps 105:39).

Second, how it pertains to their prelates particularly. Priests, whose responsibility it is to lead others to God; those who hold the law: expert teachers; shepherds: princes, whose office it is to call the people back from idolatry. The shepherds themselves have not known understanding (Isa 56:11).

Lecture 3

Infidelity and idolatry

2:9 Therefore, I shall still contend with you in judgment, says the Lord, and with your sons I will debate.

2:10 Cross over to the isles of the Cethim and see; and send to Cedar and consider diligently; and see if there is a deed of this kind:

2:11 if a nation has changed its gods. Certainly they are no gods. But my people have changed their glory into an idol.

2:12 Be astonished, O heavens, at this; and its gates be dismayed greatly, says the Lord.

2:13 For two evils has my people done: they have abandoned me, the source of living water, and they have dug for them cisterns, even broken cisterns, which are not able to hold water.

8. Here, he magnifies their guilt by noting the enormity of their misdeed.

First, he sets forth what he intends, namely, judgment: I shall still contend, concerning the enormity, as for ingratitude; with your sons, who imitate the wickedness of their fathers. The Lord will determine judgment with his people, and with Israel (Mic 6:2).

The argument follows second, when, through judgment, he shows the enormity of their sin:

first, from a comparison of religion: cross over, by consideration, to the isles of the Cethim—westerners beyond Cyprus; or a city named Cedar, where the Ishmaelites dwelt.

If a nation has changed—it is as though he were saying, “They have not changed.” From this it is certain that they are condemned by comparison with others. She has scorned my judgments, that she may be more wicked than the nations, and my precepts more than any land round about her (Ezek 5:6).

Second, when he turns from a comparison of gods and breaks forth in amazement: be astonished—it is as though he were saying, “If it were possible, the heavens would be astonished”—or the angels, who live in the heavens as the city of their king. And its gates—the chief among the angels, who act as princes and judges of the others. As in Isaiah 1:2, hear, O heavens, and give ear, O earth, for the Lord has spoken.

Next he gives the reason for his amazement. For two—against two parts of the commandment. I am the Lord your God, who brought you forth from the land of Egypt. You shall have no other gods before me. And again: you shall not make to yourselves gods of silver or gods of gold (Exod 20:3). The source of living water, who am able to give them true consolation. With you is the source of life (Ps 35:10). Cisterns—idols, which are not able to save—they have dug, because they themselves have made them. The idols of the nations are silver and gold—the works of men’s hands (Ps 113:4).

Lecture 4

Israel’s exile is due to guilt

2:14 Is Israel not my servant, or my home-born slave? Why, therefore, have you become prey?

2:15 The lions have roared over him, and sent forth their voice: they have made his land a desolation. His cities are burned, and there is none who may live in them.

2:16 The sons of Memphis and Taphnis have ravished you to the top of your head.

2:17 Was this not done to you because you abandoned the Lord your God when he led you along the way?

2:18 And now what do you wish for yourself in the way of Egypt, that you may drink muddy water? And what do you wish for yourself in the way of the Assyrians, that you may drink the water of the river?

2:19 Your wickedness will reprove you, and your turning aside will chastise you. Know and see that it is an evil and bitter thing to have left the Lord your God, and that there is no fear of him within you, says the Lord God of hosts.

9. Here, he magnifies their guilt by noting the stubbornness of their minds.

First, because they do not recognize their sin;

and second, because they add to it. As it says, from of old you have broken my yoke (Jer 2:20).

Concerning the first, there are three points.

First, he deduces the proof of their guilt from their punishments, excluding their servile condition, lest because of this they should seem to have been justly led into slavery. My home-born slave, that is, a slave born in the house of his master; it is as though he were saying, “She is not one.” We are of the seed of Abraham and we have never served anyone (John 8:33). Speaking of the variety of punishments with regard to the exile of people, why therefore have you become prey?

The lions, the princes of the Chaldeans; their voice, commands or war. With regard to the devastation of the country: they have made. Your land is desolate, your cities have been pulled down (Isa 1:7).

With regard to the rape of the women: the sons of Memphis and Taphnis, the cities of Egypt; to the top of your head—abusing her against nature and sparing no limb of her body. Or spiritually, contaminating even the strong through idolatry. They have humbled the women in Zion, and the virgins in the cities of Judah (Lam 5:11).

Then concluding with the true reason: was this not done to you because you abandoned the Lord your God? He led you, prosperously. For he did not abandon you, but you him. Because we have turned away from you, and we have not listened to the voice of the Lord our God to walk in his Law which he gave us through his servants the prophets . . . a curse has fallen upon us (Dan 9:10).

Second, he discusses the useless remedy which they had employed against punishments of this kind. From which it is clear that they did not recognize their sin, but instead hoped in the help of the nations. For this reason, he asks about the remedy: and now what do you wish for yourself? It is as though he were saying, “You should not avoid the punishments in this way, but by pleasing God,” in the way of Egypt, when he mentions the water of the Nile as a remedy, of the river, Euphrates, which belongs to the Assyrians. You who walk that you may go down to Egypt, yet have not asked at my mouth (Isa 30:2).

Their wickedness will reprove them for not having recognized their guilt, when they suffer punishment for it. The expression of their face will give judgment against them, for they have proclaimed their sin like Sodom (Isa 3:9).

Third, he offers useful advice to bring them to recognize their guilt: know . . . an evil—that it is disgraceful, bitter, and painful. The sound of the day of the Lord is bitter, then shall the strong man be troubled (Zeph 1:14).

Lecture 5

Obstinance in idolatry

2:20 From of old you have broken my yoke, you have burst my chains asunder, and you have said, ‘I will not serve.’ On every high hill, and under every leafy tree you have lain as a harlot.

10. Here, he shows that sin is multiplied by an obstinate mind:

first, with regard to duration: from of old; it is as though he were saying, “From the moment the Law was given.” I have called you a sinner from the womb (Isa 48:8). What need is there that we serve him, and what will it benefit us if we pray to him? (Job 21:15).

Second, with regard to the number of their misdeeds, on every high hill—on high and beautiful places they would sacrifice to their idols. They have sacrificed upon the mountains, and upon the hills they have reproached me (Isa 65:7). High and beautiful places are always dedicated to idols.

Lecture 6

Removal of excuses

2:21 But I have planted you as my chosen vine, every seed true: how, then, have you become for me a perversion, a foreign vine?

2:22 If you were to wash yourself with natron, and multiplied the herb berith unto yourself, still you would be stained with your iniquity before me, says the Lord.

2:23 How do you say, ‘I am not polluted; I have not walked after the Baalim’? Consider the roads in the valley and know what you have done; you are as a light runner running his paths.

2:24 As a wild ass accustomed to the wilderness, in his desire he has snuffed up the air of his love. None shall turn her. All who seek her shall not fail; in her menstruations shall they find her.

2:25 Keep back your foot from nakedness, and your throat from thirst. And you have said, ‘I have despaired, I have done wickedly; I have indeed loved others greatly, and walked after them.’

2:26 As the thief is confounded when he is caught, so are they of the house of Israel confounded, they and their kings, their princes and priests, and their prophets.

2:27 Saying to the wood, ‘You are my father,’ and to the stone, ‘You have begotten me.’ They have turned to me their back and not their face, and in the time of their affliction they will say, ‘Arise, and free us.’

2:28 Where are the gods which made you? Let them arise and free you in the time of your affliction: according to the number of your cities were your gods, O Judah.

11. Here, he removes their excuses.

First, they might raise the neglect of the husbandman. Against this he says, I have planted you as my chosen vine from elect ancestors. A vineyard was made for my beloved, on a hill fertile with oil (Isa 5:1).

Second, they might deny their guilt, at if you were to wash (Jer 2:22).

First, he convicts them of their guilt against God;

and second, of their offense against their neighbor, when he says, why do you strive to show your way good? (Jer 2:33).

Concerning the first, there are two points.

First, he convicts them of guilt when he shows them the impossibility of denying it. With natron, which is hardened by the heat of the sun, like salt, it washes what is dirty; berith, which is used for washing filth; it is an expression, as if he were saying, “All the water of a river would not make you clean,” that is, no excuse, or purgation of guilt is possible for you. A generation which seems clean to itself, yet is not washed from its filthiness (Prov 30:12).

How do you say, ‘I am not polluted’? Who is able to say that my heart is clean, I am pure from sin? (Prov 20:9).

And he considers the excuse of their guilt by setting forward two circumstances.

First, the place: consider the roads, that is, the works, in the valley, of the sons of Hinnom, where they worshiped idols. This place is watered by the springs of Siloam, and there was also a temple of Baal there, according to the Gloss. Below: direct your heart to the straight way upon which you have walked (Jer 31:21).

He also sets forth the manner of the sin.

With regard to their eagerness to sin: a light runner, as though equipped for sinning. Their feet run to evil, and make haste to shed blood (Prov 1:16).

With regard to the fervor of their lust: as a wild ass . . . in his desire—for coitus—he has snuffed the air, by which he perceives the smell of his female—of his love—of the thing loved. As it says below: the wild asses stood upon the rocks; they snuffed up the wind like dragons (Jer 14:6).

With regard to their strong attachment, none shall turn, by moving away.

And with regard to their readiness to sin: all who seek her; it is as though he were speaking of a harlot, who even in the time of her menstruations shows herself to her lovers. You have spread your legs to everyone that passed by, and you have multiplied all your fornications (Ezek 16:25).

Second, he convicts them of their hardness of mind:

first, because they are not recalled by admonitions;

second, because they are not corrected by afflictions, as it says, why do you wish to contend with me in judgment? (Jer 2:29);

third, because they are not turned by kind deeds, where it says, see the word of the Lord (Jer 2:31).

Concerning the first, there are three points.

First, he gives the admonition: keep back; it is as though he were saying, “Cease from the sins for which you will be led barefoot and thirsting into captivity.” As it says in Isaiah: as my servant Isaiah has walked naked and without shoes, so the king of Assyria will threaten the captivity of Egypt, and the exile of Ethiopia, the young men and the old, naked and barefoot, with uncovered buttocks to the shame of Egypt (Isa 20:3).

Second, he shows the rebellion of the people: and you have said, ‘I have despaired, I have done wickedly.’ Others, idols. Below: we have no hope, we shall go after our own thoughts, and each one of us will do according to the perversity of his own wicked heart (Jer 18:12).

Third, he threatens them with shame.

First, he sets forth an example of shame. As the thief is confounded when he is caught, so are they of the house of Israel confounded, caught in punishments.

Saying to the wood—to a wooden idol—‘You are my father’—my god. Let all those who worship graven images be ashamed, who glory in their idols (Ps 96:7). Let them be confounded by their idols (Isa 1:29).

Second, he lays out the order in which their shame will come.

And he discusses, first, the loss of divine assistance: they have turned, as though disobedient. They have turned their back and gone away, they have made their ears heavy lest they hear the Law (Zech 7:11). In the time of their affliction. In their tribulation they shall rise early to me. Come let us return to the Lord. (Hos 6:1).

Second, he discusses the uselessness of their idolatry. Where are the gods? Where are their gods, in which they had placed their confidence? (Deut 32:37).

Third, the multitude of their idols: according to the number of your cities were your gods, O Judah. The land is filled with horses, and his innumerable chariots of war (Isa 2:8).

Lecture 7

Vain correction

2:29 Why do you wish to contend with me in judgment? You have all left me, says the Lord.

2:30 In vain have I struck your children; they have not received correction. Your sword has swallowed up your prophets; like a wasting lion is your generation.

12. Here, he shows their stubbornness, insofar as they are not corrected by chastisements.

First, he is astonished at their excuse: why do you wish to contend with me in judgment? If one would wish to contend with him, he would not be able to answer him one time in a thousand (Job 9:3).

Second, he shows how insensible they are of lesser afflictions: in vain have I struck your children, and your elders. Your sword has swallowed up: by this he means the enemy, which they have justly deserved, who has been sent to destroy them. Your prophets, that is, the false prophets of Baal, and the soothsayers. And speaking of them all as a group he says: like a wasting lion, he lays waste; or like a wasting lion, he is destroyed, for all rise up to kill a lion, or a wolf. As it says below: in vain has the founder melted, for their wickedness is not consumed. Reprobate silver you shall call them, because the Lord has cast them off (Jer 6:29–30).

Lecture 8

Refusal of kindnesses

2:31 See the word of the Lord: have I become a wilderness to Israel, or a barren land? Why, therefore, has my people said, ‘We have withdrawn; we will come no more to you’?

2:32 Can a virgin forget her ornament, or a bride the fillet about her breast? But my people have forgotten me days without number.

13. Here, he shows their hardness from the fact that they are not turned by kindness.

First, he puts forward those kindnesses which pertain to the carrying on of life: see, that is, understand, have I become a wilderness to Israel? Indeed, I am like a city and a fruitful land for you, in that I defend you and give increase to the land: he will come to us as the early and the latter rains (Hos 6:3).

Second, he speaks of those things which pertain to apparel: can a virgin forget her ornament? He has girded me about with the robe of justice (Isa 56:10).

Lecture 9

Murder of the poor

2:33 Why do you strive to show your way good to find love? Moreover you have taught malice to be your ways.

2:34 And on your wings is the blood of the souls of the poor and the innocent found? I have not found them in ditches, but in every place which I mentioned above.

2:35 And you said, ‘I am without sin and innocent, and so let your wrath be turned away from me.’ Behold, I will contend with you in judgment, because you said, ‘I have not sinned.’

2:36a How exceedingly vile have you become, going the same ways over again!

14. Here, he accuses them of crimes against their neighbors.

First, he proves their guilt;

second, he threatens the punishment of confusion where he says, and you shall be confounded by Egypt (Jer 2:36b).

Concerning the first, there are three points.

First, he brings up the murder of their neighbors, and marvels at their excuse: to find love, as though through these things you could appease me. He then speaks of spiritual murder: moreover you have taught, by evil example. As it says below: you have taught them against yourself, and instructed them against your own head (Jer 13:21).

He also speaks of physical murder: on your wings, as in bird wings—he is using the metaphor of a bird of prey. The poor, whom you have sacrificed to idols. I looked and behold, two women coming forth, and the wind was in their wings, and they had wings like the wings of a kite (Zech 5:9).

Second, the manifestation of their guilt: I have not found them in ditches, as though secretly murdered by thieves, but in every hill and pleasant place. Truth has fallen in the street, and righteousness cannot enter. Truth has been forgotten, and he who withdraws from evil is laid open as prey (Isa 59:14).

And he marvels at their excuse: and you said. And you said my speech is pure, I am clean in your sight (Job 11:4).

Third, he repeats their guilt, this time passing over his surprise at their excuse: behold, I.

How exceeding vile have you become, going the same ways over again, that is, returning to their sins. See, O Lord, and consider, for I am become vile (Lam 1:11).

Lecture 10

Confounded by Egypt

2:36b And you shall be confounded by Egypt, as you were confounded by Assyria.

2:37 For you will go forth from her, and your hands shall be upon your head, because the Lord has destroyed your confidence, and you shall have no prosperity in it.

15. Here, he threatens them with the punishment of humiliation: and you shall be confounded by Egypt, on whose help you hoped, by Assyria, because you sought the help of Egypt against the ten tribes, and the Assyrians.

Your hands shall be upon your head, as a sign of grief. The strength of pharaoh shall be shame to you, and your trust in the shadow of Egypt, disgrace (Isa 30:3).

Collations

Note that here sin is called vanity (Jer 2:5), because it chooses what is only imaginary. Blessed is the man whose hope is in the name of the Lord, who does not consider vanities and false hopes (Ps 30:5). Because it considers permanent what is only transitory. Their days have departed in vanity, and their years with haste (Ps 78:33). Because it is deceitful in its expectation. Vain hope and a lie belong to a senseless man, and dreams lift up fools (Sir 34:1). Because its results bear no fruit. In vain have I labored, I have spent my strength without cause, and in vain (Isa 49:4).

One must also note that sacramental purity is a source of water (Jer 2:13): after these waters shall have come there, everything shall be healed and live, wherever the torrent comes (Ezek 47:9). The waters of restored doctrine: let all who thirst come to the waters, and those without money, make haste, buy and eat (Isa 55:1). The waters of restoring grace: it will become in him a well of water springing up to eternal life (John 4:14). The waters of resplendent glory: he showed me a river of living water, shining like crystal, coming forth from the throne of God and of the Lamb (Rev 22:1).

Chapter 3

Israel and Judah as adulteresses

Lecture 1

Mercy upon the adulteress

3:1 It is said commonly, if a man shall send his wife away, and she, departing from him, marry another man, can he return to her again? Will not that woman be unclean and defiled? Though you have fornicated with your many lovers, still, return to me, says the Lord, and I will take you.

3:2 Lift up your eyes on high, and see where you have not lain. You would sit by the roads waiting them as a thief in the desert; you have defiled the land with your fornications, and your iniquities.

3:3 Therefore, the drops of the rains were held back, and the latter rain was not given. You have had the forehead of a harlot, you would not blush.

3:4 Therefore, at least from this time forward cry unto me, ‘You are my Father, the guide of my youth.

3:5a Will you be angry forever, or will you continue to the end?’

16. Here, he calls them to repentance.

First, he speaks of the invitation to return.

Second, he speaks of the stubbornness of those who are called back: and you shall say to them (Jer 8:4).

The first is divided into two.

In the first, he speaks of the admonitions to return;

and in the second, the reasons for the same: if you return, O Israel, says the Lord, to turn back to me (Jer 4:1).

The first point is divided into two.

In the first, he calls the people back;

in the second, he mentions the fruit of their repudiation, when he says: but as a woman that despises her lover (Jer 3:20).

The first point is divided into two.

In the first, he calls them back from the crime of adultery;

in the second, from its punishment, where he says: go and shout these words against the north wind (Jer 3:12).

The first part is divided into two.

In the first, he calls them back with admonitions;

in the second, by examples, at and the Lord said to me in the days of Josiah the king (Jer 3:6a).

Concerning the first, he does two things.

First, he calls them back by reason of the mercy of God, which he proves by citing the common proverb: if a man shall send his wife away, which comes from Deuteronomy 24:1: if a man shall send his wife away. He speaks according to the permission given regarding the bill of divorce because of the hardness of their heart (Matt 19:8); he was forbidding a return either so that they would not be readily prone to divorce, or lest they kill her after having taken her back with deceit. From this proverb he draws the proof of divine clemency: you have fornicated with your many lovers, the idols. Let him return to the Lord and he will have mercy on him, and to our God, because he will abundantly pardon (Isa 55:7).

Second, he calls them back from their guilt.

First, by exposing their guilt, and indicating the place where it occurred: lift up your eyes on high, that is, with right consideration, or alternatively, “everywhere”; where have you not, as though everywhere, lain, like a harlot, for idolatry. You have made a brothel in every street (Ezek 16:24). And the desire for sinning: you would sit by the roads, like a harlot waiting for her lovers, and as a thief waiting for those whom he might despoil. And behold, there met him a woman with the attire of a harlot, prepared to deceive souls (Prov 7:10). Gilead is a city of those who make idols, supplanted with blood, and like the jaws of highway robbers (Hos 6:8). He indicates the effect of their sin: you have defiled the land. As we saw above, you polluted my land, and turned my inheritance into an abomination (Jer 2:7).

Then, he establishes the punishment for the sin: therefore, the drops of the rains were held back, by whose power the rain and the storm are generated—I shall command the clouds that they rain not showers upon my vine (Isa 5:6). And he shows the shamelessness of those who sinned, the forehead of a harlot. As it says below, nay; rather, they were not ashamed of shame (Jer 6:15).

Second, he urges them to repentance.

First, he encourages them to recognize the benefits: therefore. It is as though he were saying, “The past suffices, and so on.” You are my Father, through creation, the guide of my youth. It is as though he were saying, “When you led me through the desert, I was yet a virgin.” Who forsook the guide of her youth (Prov 2:17). The Spirit of the Lord was her guide (Isa 63:14).

Second, he urges them to seek mercy, and says: will you be angry forever, or will you continue to the end? Will you be angry with us forever, or extend your anger from generation to generation? (Ps 84:6).

Lecture 2

Israel’s contempt

3:5b Behold you have spoken, and you have done wickedness, and you were able.

3:6a The Lord said to me in the days of Josiah the king: have you not seen what rebellious Israel has done?

17. Here, he shows their contempt with regard to the sins of the mouth: behold you have spoken, which refers to prideful words, etc. With regard to sins of commission: and you have done, which corresponds to the phrase, you have satisfied wickedness, with regard to the sin of the heart; and you were able—to which he adds, and you have displayed, as though you reveled in your power to sin. Now the phrase, and for your words, is not in the Hebrew, or in ancient books, but appears to be a clarification of Jerome. God gave him a space to repent, but he abused it in his pride (Job 24:23). Woe to you who are mighty to drink wine, and strong men to mix drunkenness (Isa 5:22).

Lecture 3

Judah and Israel’s adultery

3:6b She has gone away upon every high mountain, and under every green tree, and fornicated there.

3:7 And I said when she had done all these things, ‘Return to me,’ but she did not return. And Judah her treacherous sister saw,

3:8 that because rebellious Israel had committed adultery, I sent her away, and gave her a bill of divorce. Yet treacherous Judah her sister did not fear, but strayed and even fornicated herself.

3:9 With the heedlessness of her fornication she polluted the earth, and committed adultery with stone and wood:

3:10 In all these things, her treacherous sister Judah did not return to me with her whole heart, but with a lie, says the Lord.

3:11 And the Lord said to me, rebellious Israel has justified her soul, by comparing herself to treacherous Judah.

18. Here, he calls them back by the example of the ten tribes: and concerning this he makes three points.

First, he gives an example of the crime of idolatry—she has gone away, following her own inclinations, and not my commands;

and he speaks of her disobedience: and I said. They have made for themselves statues, and groves on every high hill, and under every leafy tree, and there they burned incense upon their altars after the manner of the nations (2 Kgs 17:10). And as for the punishment which he made known to them: her treacherous sister saw, because they arose from the same ancestors.

I gave her a bill of divorce: she maintains the likeness of a harlot, in whom filthiness is found.

Yet, on the contrary, what is this bill of divorce? (Isa 50:1).

It must be said that God, insofar as it pertains to him, divorces no one, but that it is sinful man who divorces himself from God. For this reason, it says in the same place: you have been sold for your iniquities, and for your wickedness have I divorced your mother.

Second, he exposes their contempt: yet treacherous Judah her sister did not fear.

With stone, that is, with an idol made of stone. But neither did Judah himself keep the commandments of the Lord his God, but erred and strayed in the errors which Israel had done (2 Kgs 17:19).

Third, he exacerbates their guilt: and the Lord said to me, rebellious Israel has justified, comparatively, because the ten tribes had not seen the punishment of other peoples, and they were not corrected. You have justified your sisters in all your abominations, which you have done (Ezek 16:51).

Lecture 4

Call to return

3:12 Go and shout these words against the north wind, and say: return rebellious Israel, says the Lord, and I will not turn my face away from you, because I am holy, says the Lord, and I will not be angry forever.

3:13 But recognize your iniquity, that you have rebelled against the Lord your God, and scattered your ways to strangers under every green tree, and have not listened to my voice, says the Lord.

3:14a Turn back, returning children, says the Lord, because I am your husband.

19. Here, he calls them back from the punishment of captivity.

First, he calls them back.

Second, he shows them the cause of their being called back, at but I said (Jer 3:19).

Concerning the first, there are two points.

First, he speaks of their being called back;

and second, the response of those who were called back: and I will take you, one from a city and two from a family, and I will lead you into Zion (Jer 3:14b).

Concerning the first, there are two points.

First, he calls them back from the ten tribes when he calls them to return, against the north wind, where they are held captive in the land of the Assyrians. He will not always be angry, nor threaten forever (Ps 102:9).

And regarding the recognition of their sins, but recognize. You have . . . scattered your ways to strangers, as though to many idols. As we saw above, consider the roads in the valley and know what you have done (Jer 2:23).

Second, speaking of those from the two tribes, turn back, by repentance, returning, to me. Or, returning, from captivity. And it shall be on that day, says the Lord, that she will call me, ‘My husband,’ and will no more call upon the Baalim (Hos 2:16).

Lecture 5

Promise of shepherds

3:14b And I will take you, one from a city and two from a family, and I will lead you into Zion.

3:15 And I will give you shepherds after my own heart, and they will feed you with knowledge and understanding.

3:16 And when you have multiplied, and grown numerous in the land in those days, says the Lord, they shall not say anymore, the ark of the covenant of the Lord, neither shall it come into their heart, neither shall there be remembrance of it, neither shall it be visited, or be anymore.

3:17 In that time they will call Jerusalem the throne of the Lord; and all nations shall be gathered together in her in the name of the Lord in Jerusalem, and they will not walk after the depravity of their own wicked hearts.

3:18 In those days the house of Judah shall go forth to the house of Israel, and together they shall come from the land of the north to the land which I gave to your fathers.

20. Here, he takes up the response of those who have been called back:

first, those of the two tribes, promising them the restitution of their native land: one from a city, because they were scattered, or because not all have come back. Indeed, I will take you from the nations, and I will gather you from every land, and I will lead you to your own land (Ezek 36:24).

And the restoration of justice by the correction of their rulers: and I will give you shepherds after my own heart, and they will feed you with knowledge, regarding faith, and understanding, regarding morals. I will restore your judges as they were before, and your counsellors as they were of old (Isa 1:26).

Third, with regard to the increase of glory, which he signifies by the forgetting of what has come before: when you have multiplied . . . they shall not say anymore, the ark of the covenant of the Lord—as though they will account what they now glory in as nothing, because the ark had been hidden and not found, as it says in 2 Maccabees 1.

And from the promise of future things: in that time they will call Jerusalem, whole, because it has been completed by the reputation for sanctity in which it was held, as it says in 1 Maccabees 3. The place of my throne, and the place of the prints of my feet, where I dwell in the midst of the children of Israel forever (Ezek 43:7).

And with regard to the increase of worship: and all nations shall be gathered together, some will come from all peoples, for they shall come to pray in Jerusalem. Or it may be understood to speak of the conversion of the nations to the faith of Christ. All the nations, which you have made, will come and worship before you, O Lord (Ps 85:9).

Second, he discusses the conversion of the ten tribes: in those days the house of Judah shall go forth to the house of Israel, because those who returned were joined to the two tribes. This is alluded to by the fact that it is said: together they shall come. As it is said below: in those days, and at that time, says the Lord, the children of Israel shall come, they and the children of Judah together, walking and weeping they shall make haste, and shall seek the Lord their God (Jer 50:4). Some, however, interpret all this to refer to the last times.

Lecture 6

Reason for mercy

3:19 But I said: how shall I set you among the sons, and give you a desirable land, an excellent inheritance from among the hosts of the nations? And I said: you will call me ‘Father,’ and you will not cease to walk after me.

21. Here, he gives the reason for their being called back.

First, he poses the question: how? That is, how is it possible that I should bestow so many good things upon you, when you languish in so many sins? Among the sons, that is, among a multitude of sons, the hosts of the nations, that is, where, since ancient times, armies have gathered, as they do even to this day, because of its allurements: they set at naught the desirable land (Ps 105:24). The lines have fallen to me in goodly places, for my inheritance is goodly to me (Ps 15:6).

Second, he gives the response: and I said, that is, foreseeing the good which you will do, I promise these things to you. He will call upon me: you are my father, my God and the support of my salvation (Ps 88:27).

Lecture 7

Shame after conversion

3:20 But as a woman that despises her lover, so has the house of Israel despised me, says the Lord.

3:21 A voice was heard on the roads, the crying and the wailing of the children of Israel, for they have made their way wicked, and have forgotten the Lord their God.

3:22 Turn back, wayward children, and I will heal your backslidings. Behold, we have come to you, for you are the Lord our God.

3:23 Surely the hills were deceitful, the multitude of the mountains; truly in the Lord our God is the salvation of Israel.

3:24 Shame has devoured the labor of our fathers from our youth, their flocks and their herds, their sons and their daughters.

3:25 We will sleep in our shame, and our disgrace shall cover us, for we have sinned against the Lord our God, we and our fathers, from our youth until this day, and we have not listened to the voice of the Lord our God.

22. Here he discusses the fruit of their return.

First, he speaks of the necessity of their being called back as regards their own guilt—lover, not husband. Go, and love a woman loved by a lover, an adulteress (Hos 3:1).

Regarding the punishment, a voice was heard on the roads. As it says below, a voice was heard on high: lamentation, grief and wailing, Rachel weeping for her children (Jer 31:15).

Second, he discusses the usefulness of conversion: turn back, as it says above, backslidings, the things by which you have been turned away from God. Who heals all your infirmities (Ps 102:3). Turn back to me, and you will be saved (Isa 45:22).

Third, he treats of their return.
